name: Cross-legged
summary: The default floor seat: shins crossed, knees dropping toward the floor. Comfortable for most people once the hips learn to open — and the single most useful position to make friends with first.
demands: Hip external rotation (the knees fall out, not the ankles up)
A pelvis that can stay upright rather than rolling back
cues: Sit on the front edge of a firm cushion so the hips are higher than the knees — this tips the pelvis forward and un-rounds the low back
Let the knees be heavy; don't force them down
Stack the shins loosely rather than jamming the feet under the thighs
Swap which shin is in front every few minutes
cautions: If the hips are tight, the pelvis tucks under and the load shifts to the lumbar spine and knees — raise the seat higher instead of muscling through
Alternate the front leg; a habitual side builds asymmetry

name: Kneeling
summary: Kneeling with the hips settled back over the heels. Tall, alert, and surprisingly restful — the traditional Japanese sitting posture, and the one that keeps the spine stacked with the least effort.
demands: Knee flexion and ankle plantarflexion (pointing the toes back)
Tolerance for weight through the front of the shins
cues: Slide a seiza bench or a firm cushion between your heels to take the load off the ankles and knees
Let the sit-bones rest down; lengthen up through the crown
Point the toes straight back, not tucked
Come out and shake the legs the moment they tingle
cautions: Seiza reduces blood flow in the lower leg — pins-and-needles is normal and harmless, but don't hold it long enough to go fully numb
Skip or heavily prop it with knee pain or after a knee/ankle injury

name: Resting squat
summary: Heels down, hips low, chest tall — the resting position much of the world still drops into to wait, cook, and chat. The best single test and builder of ankle and hip range you own.
demands: Roughly 35° of ankle dorsiflexion (the calf and soleus have to lengthen)
Deep hip flexion with the knees tracking over the toes
cues: Start heels-elevated on a rolled mat, a wedge, or a couple of books — this unlocks the squat while the ankles open up
Feet a little wider than the hips, toes turned slightly out
Push the knees gently apart with the elbows and lift the chest
Hold for short, frequent bouts rather than one long marathon
cautions: Prolonged, high-volume deep squatting is linked to more knee osteoarthritis — keep it varied and pain-free rather than grinding out long holds
Back out of any sharp knee pain; build the range slowly over weeks
props: A wall or door frame to hold

name: Long-sitting
summary: Legs straight out in front, torso tall. A clean counter-pose to all the folded positions — it asks the hamstrings to lengthen and the spine to hold itself up.
demands: Hamstring length (short hamstrings pull the pelvis into a slump)
Active trunk extension to stay upright
cues: Sit up on a cushion so the pelvis can tip forward
Soften the knees rather than locking them
Press the backs of the thighs down and grow tall through the spine
A slight bend in the knees is fine — height matters more than straight legs
cautions: Tight hamstrings round the low back — raise the seat and bend the knees before forcing the legs flat

name: Side-sitting
summary: Both knees folded to one side — a relaxed, informal way to be on the floor that most people already fall into. Its whole trick is remembering to switch sides.
demands: External rotation in the front hip, internal rotation in the back hip
cues: Let the whole pelvis settle; don't perch on one buttock
Keep the ribs stacked over the hips rather than collapsing sideways
Deliberately switch to the other side every few minutes
Use a hand or cushion to level the low hip
cautions: It loads the two sides unequally — habitually favouring one side deepens an asymmetry, so alternate on purpose
Ease off if the knee of the back leg complains

name: Between the heels
summary: Kneeling with the feet apart and the hips settling down between the heels. A deep, quad-and-ankle stretch that doubles as a very stable, upright seat once the knees allow it.
demands: Deep knee flexion with tolerance for the tibia rotating
cues: Sit up on a block or bolster placed between the feet — never force the hips to the floor
Keep the feet pointing straight back, not splaying out
Lengthen up; let the thighs and ankles stretch, not the knees
Build tolerance with short holds
cautions: This is the highest-knee-stress seat here — prop it generously and avoid it entirely with knee pain or injury
Sharp knee pain means come out; a broad quad/ankle stretch is the goal
goodFor: A tall, stable seat once available
props: Block or bolster between the feet
